attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- algorithmic-art: Creating algorithmic art using p5.js with seeded randomness and interactive parameter exploration. Use this when users request creating art using code, generative art, algorithmic art, flow fields, or particle systems. Create original algorithmic art rather than copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- brand-guidelines: Applies Anthropic's official brand colors and typography to any sort of artifact that may benefit from having Anthropic's look-and-feel. Use it when brand colors or style guidelines, visual formatting, or company design standards apply.
- canvas-design: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. You should use this skill when the user asks to create a poster, piece of art, design, or other static piece. Create original visual designs, never copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. TRIGGER when: code imports anthropic/@anthropic-ai/sdk; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (/v1/agents, /v1/sessions, /v1/environments). DO NOT TRIGGER when: code imports `openai`/other AI SDK, general programming, or ML/data-science tasks.
- doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- internal-comms: A set of resources to help me write all kinds of internal communications, using the formats that my company likes to use. Claude should use this skill whenever asked to write some sort of internal communications (status reports, leadership updates, 3P updates, company newsletters, FAQs, incident reports, project updates, etc.).
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase validate/gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- slack-gif-creator: Knowledge and utilities for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack. Provides constraints, validation tools, and animation concepts. Use when users request animated GIFs for Slack like "make me a GIF of X doing Y for Slack."
- theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- web-artifacts-builder: Suite of tools for creating elaborate, multi-component claude.ai HTML artifacts using modern frontend web technologies (React, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui). Use for complex artifacts requiring state management, routing, or shadcn/ui components - not for simple single-file HTML/JSX artifacts.
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.

- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- statusline: Set up Claude Code's status line UI
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch
- insights: Generate a report analyzing your Claude Code sessions
- team-onboarding: Help teammates ramp on Claude Code with a guide from your usage

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive summary of all the files and their purposes.

## Summary of libnn-optimizer Skill Files and Structure

Based on my comprehensive search of the agentic_libnn_opt project, here is the complete file structure and what each file does:

### **Core Configuration & State Files**

1. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/config.toml`**
   - TOML configuration file for the optimization workflow
   - Contains toolchain paths, target CPU (AX45MPV), QEMU binary path, FPGA connectivity (host:port), and optimization limits
   - Specifies FPGA config file path and resource limits (max iterations, minimum improvement threshold)

2. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/state.json`**
   - JSON state persistence file tracking optimization progress across sessions
   - Records: current mode (single_function or model), function being optimized, source input file, current phase
   - Tracks baseline performance, best achieved results, and full history of all iterations with accepted/rejected status
   - Each history entry includes iteration number, git commit SHA, cycle/instruction counts, and optimization notes

### **Documentation & Planning**

3. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/CLAUDE.md`**
   - Project overview and setup guide for Claude Code
   - Documents libnn library architecture, function naming conventions, build/test commands
   - Explains QEMU testing with strace mode for semihosting, FPGA performance testing procedures
   - Details multi-variant implementation patterns (Vector/DSP/Plain C), compilation flags, and commit message conventions
   - Key info: AX45MPV is primary target with VLEN=1024

4. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/prompt.md`**
   - High-level requirements for the autonomous optimization workflow
   - Specifies two modes: single function optimization and model-level optimization
   - Defines deliverables (roadmap and implementation plan)

5. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/plan.md`**
   - Detailed 14-step implementation plan for the optimization skill
   - Documents the five optimization phases: gather, baseline, iterate, evaluate, done
   - Defines state file schema, directory structure, and verifiable tests for each step
   - Explains macro-guarding pattern: new optimized code paths guarded by `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` macros
   - Details Phase 1-5 workflows including parsing tasks, baseline measurement, iteration loop, and final evaluation

### **Python Scripts & Validation**

6. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/script/validate_config.py`**
   - 450-line Python script validating the optimization environment setup
   - Performs 7 sequential validation steps with fail-fast behavior:
     1. Loads and verifies all required config.toml keys
     2. Validates toolchain (bin_dir exists, gcc present, Andes flags supported)
     3. Compiles a hello world C program
     4. Tests QEMU with the compiled hello world (with optional strace mode for fd-9 capture)
     5. Parses FPGA .inc config file to extract hardware features
     6. Checks FPGA board TCP connectivity
     7. Runs compiled program on actual FPGA board
   - Color-coded output (green [OK], red [FAIL], yellow [WARN])
   - Exports helper functions for parsing Verilog defines and FPGA config extraction

7. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/script/test/test_validate_config.py`**
   - Comprehensive test suite (250+ lines) for validate_config.py
   - Uses unittest.mock for unit testing individual steps
   - Tests cover: config loading, toolchain validation, compilation, QEMU execution, FPGA config parsing
   - Verifies FPGA connectivity checks, command-line argument parsing
   - Ensures test values match actual config.toml and hardware setup

### **Hardware Configuration**

8. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/fpga_config/ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc`**
   - Verilog include file defining AX45MPV FPGA configuration
   - Contains Verilog `define` statements for:
     - Core parameters: CPUID, VLEN=1024, DLEN=1024, ELEN=64
     - Cache hierarchy: D-cache=32KB/4-way, I-cache=32KB, L2=8192KB/16-way, cache_line=64B
     - ISA extensions: RVV support, vector dot product, BFloat16, INT4 vector load, QMAC
     - Memory bandwidth: BIU=512-bit, L2=512-bit
   - Used by validation script to extract hardware features and guide optimization decisions

### **Directory Structure**

9. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/build/`**
   - Output directory for compiled libnn.a libraries during optimization iterations
   - Holds both baseline and optimized builds for A/B comparison

10. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/patches/`**
    - Audit trail directory storing diff patches for each iteration
    - Per-file: `iter_<N>_<func>.patch` captures what changed in each attempt

11. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/log/`**
    - Build/test/profiling logs per iteration for debugging and reference

12. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/.claude/skills/optimize/`**
    - Designated skills directory location (currently empty - for future skill definition)

### **Supporting Root-Level Scripts**

13. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/build.sh`**
    - Main build script for libnn library
    - Accepts CPU target and optional compiler flags
    - Called by validation and optimization phases

14. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/test_perf.sh`**
    - FPGA performance profiling script
    - Generates performance.csv with per-function cycle and instruction counts
    - Primary tool for measuring optimization impact

15. **`/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/test.sh`**
    - Unit testing script supporting QEMU and board validation modes

### **Key Design Patterns**

The libnn-optimizer skill follows these principles:
- **State-driven**: JSON state file enables resumable multi-session optimization
- **Macro-guarded paths**: Optimizations added under preprocessor conditionals without modifying baseline
- **Hardware-aware**: FPGA config parsing drives tiling and vectorization decisions
- **Validation-first**: Config validation catches toolchain/connectivity issues before optimization starts
- **Audit trail**: All iterations tracked in state.json history with git commit SHAs for reproducibility

file.content:
# Prompt: Autonomous Agentic libnn Optimization Workflow

## Goal

Design and implement an autonomous agentic optimization workflow for libnn.

## Two Optimization Modes

1. **Single function** - Optimize a single libnn function with specified input shapes.
2. **Model-level** - Optimize a set of libnn functions called by a given AI model, each with fixed input shapes.

## Target Hardware

- Optimization targets a specified FPGA configuration file (Verilog `.inc` format, e.g., `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc`).
- Extract target features (VLEN, cache sizes, ISA extensions, etc.) from the FPGA config.

## User Configuration File

Allow the user to specify:
- Toolchain path
- Target architecture
- QEMU path
- FPGA address (domain:port)

## Deliverables

- `roadmap.md` - High-level vision document.
- `plan.md` - Step-by-step implementation plan for Claude Code, clear for both human and LLM readers.

## Constraints

- Each step contains exactly one feature.
- Each step has verifiable test cases.
- Each step includes: feature name, description, key files to modify, and dependencies on other steps.

content.content:
1	# Implementation Plan: Autonomous Agentic libnn Optimization Workflow
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	libnn functions have multi-variant C implementations (plain C / DSP / RVV). Optimizing a function means writing a better RVV implementation for a specific FPGA target. Currently this is manual: read source, write code, build, test on QEMU, profile on FPGA, iterate.
6	
7	This plan defines how **Claude Code directly drives the optimization loop** -- no Python orchestration framework. The LLM reads source files, parses hardware configs, writes optimized C code, and calls existing shell scripts for build/test/profile. For state persistence across sessions, a simple JSON file tracks progress.
8	
9	The optimization is specialized for a **designated target configuration** (CPU, VLEN, cache hierarchy, ISA extensions) and **known static-time input shapes** (batch size, spatial dimensions, channel counts, kernel sizes, etc.) extracted from existing test/example C files. Dynamic shapes are not supported -- each optimization run targets a fixed set of input dimensions, enabling aggressive tiling, unrolling, and register allocation decisions that would not be valid for arbitrary shapes.
10	
11	**The baseline code path is never modified.** Instead, the agent edits the same source file (e.g., `/home/nick/work/libnn/Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c`) and adds one or more optimized paths, each guarded by its own macro. The baseline path remains byte-for-byte intact under `#else`. A/B comparison is a rebuild with or without the relevant `-D` flag.
12	
13	Macro naming:
14	- **Single mode:** one macro per function, `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>` (e.g. `ENA_AGENT_OPT_CONV_DW_HWC_U8_U8_U8_ASYM_BIAS_ANY`).
15	- **Model mode:** the same function can appear multiple times with different shape-sets, and each shape-set gets its own specialized path. Macros are suffixed by shape-set index: `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>_S0`, `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>_S1`, ... Dispatch at the top of the function picks the right specialization by comparing the incoming shape arguments against the compile-time constants of each shape-set; if none match, fall through to the baseline `#else`. The index-to-shape-set mapping is recorded in `state.json` so the build flags and dispatcher stay in sync.
16	
17	The FPGA configuration (Verilog `.inc` file like `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc`) provides the ground truth for target features: VLEN, DLEN, L2 cache size, ISA extensions, etc. Cache sizes are especially important since the library's tiling GEMM currently hardcodes L1=512KB -- the actual L2=8MB and L1 D-cache=32KB from the config should drive tiling parameter selection.
18	
19	## Two Modes of Operation
20	
21	### Mode 1: Single Function
22	
23	**Input:** A performance test file from `Examples/unit_performance/`, e.g.:
24	```
25	Examples/unit_performance/t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c
26	```
27	
28	The LLM reads this file and extracts:
29	- **Function name** from the `riscv_nn_*()` call site
30	- **All static input shapes** from the `#define` macros at the top of the file (`INPUT_X=16`, `INPUT_Y=15`, `INPUT_CH=4`, `KERNEL_X=2`, `CH_MULT=192`, `STRIDE_X=1`, `PAD_X=0`, etc.)
31	- **Quantization parameters** (`OUT_SHIFT`, `OUT_MULT`, `INPUT_OFFSET`, `FILTER_OFFSET`, `OUTPUT_OFFSET`, activation min/max)
32	- **Data types** inferred from the function name suffix (`u8_u8_u8`, `s8_s8_s8`, etc.)
33	
34	No separate TOML or config file needed -- the perf test file *is* the task specification.
35	
36	### Mode 2: Complete Model
37	
38	**Input:** A model example C file, e.g.:
39	```
40	Examples/ARM/arm_nn_examples/cifar10/nds_nn_examples_cifar10.c
41	```
42	
43	The LLM reads the model file and its companion parameter header (found via `#include`) to extract the full op sequence with concrete shapes:
44	
45	| # | Function | Key shapes |
46	|---|----------|-----------|
47	| 1 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_RGB_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=32, im_ch=3, ker=5, out_ch=32, stride=1, pad=2 |
48	| 2 | `relu_s8` | size=32*32*32 |
49	| 3 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=32, ch=32, ker=3, stride=2 |
50	| 4 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=16, im_ch=32, ker=5, out_ch=16 |
51	| 5 | `relu_s8` | size=16*16*16 |
52	| 6 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=16, ch=16, ker=3, stride=2 |
53	| 7 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=8, im_ch=16, ker=5, out_ch=32 |
54	| 8 | `relu_s8` | size=8*8*32 |
55	| 9 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=8, ch=32, ker=3, stride=2 |
56	| 10 | `fc_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | in_dim=512, out=10 |
57	| 11 | `softmax_s8_fast` | size=10 |
58	
59	The LLM profiles all (function, shape-set) pairs and optimizes every one of them to reach the best total model cycle count. Cost per pair is measured directly -- baseline cycles at that exact shape, multiplied by how many times the model invokes the function with that shape. Same function with two different shape sets = two independent pairs with independent costs (a 32x32 conv and an 8x8 conv share a name but not a cycle budget). Ranking by per-pair total cycles only sets work *order* so the largest wins land first and model-level cycles drop monotonically; it does not gate coverage. The long tail is still specialized -- a small kernel invoked many times can dominate once the heavy convs are tuned.
60	
61	## Directory Structure
62	
63	```
64	agentic_libnn_opt/
65	  plan.md                                   # This file
66	  opt/
67	    config.toml                             # Target config (toolchain, FPGA, QEMU paths)
68	    state.json                              # Optimization state (persists across sessions)
69	    patches/                                # Per-iteration diffs against libnn Source/ (audit trail)
70	      iter_<N>_<func>.patch
71	    log/                                    # Build/test/perf logs per iteration
72	  # The agent's macro for the new path:
73	  #   -DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]            enables the optimized code path in Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c
74	  ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc              # FPGA Verilog config (input)
75	```
76	
77	## Configuration
78	
79	Single TOML file at `opt/config.toml` -- read directly by the LLM, no schema validation layer needed:
80	
81	```toml
82	[toolchain]
83	bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
84	prefix = "riscv64-elf-"
85	
86	[target]
87	cpu = "AX45MPV"
88	fpga_config = "ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc"
89	
90	[qemu]
91	binary = "/local/nick/qemu_andes/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
92	vlen = 1024
93	timeout = 120
94	
95	[fpga]
96	host = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
97	port = 1116
98	timeout = 300
99	
100	[limits]
101	max_iterations = 10
102	min_improvement_pct = 2.0
103	```
104	
105	## State File
106	
107	`opt/state.json` -- simple JSON the LLM reads/writes to track progress across sessions:
108	
109	```json
110	{
111	  "mode": "single_function",
112	  "function": "conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any",
113	  "source_input": "Examples/unit_performance/t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c",
114	  "phase": "evaluate",
115	  "iteration": 3,
116	  "baseline": { "cycles": 45200, "instructions": 32100 },
117	  "best": { "cycles": 38900, "instructions": 28500, "iteration": 2, "commit": "e4f5a6b" },
118	  "history": [
119	    { "iteration": 1, "commit": "a1b2c3d", "cycles": 42000, "instructions": 30200, "accepted": true,  "notes": "unrolled inner loop 4x" },
120	    { "iteration": 2, "commit": "e4f5a6b", "cycles": 38900, "instructions": 28500, "accepted": true,  "notes": "tiled for L1 dcache 32KB" },
121	    { "iteration": 3, "commit": "9c8d7e6", "cycles": 39100, "instructions": 27800, "accepted": false, "notes": "register pressure regression" }
122	  ]
123	}
124	```
125	
126	Field semantics:
127	- `mode`: `"single_function"` or `"model"`. Drives which loop variant runs.
128	- `function`: bare function name (no `riscv_nn_` prefix). Identifies which baseline `.c` gets the new macro-guarded path.
129	- `source_input`: path to the perf test `.c` (single mode) or model `.c` (model mode). The task spec.
130	- `phase`: current loop phase (`gather`, `baseline`, `iterate`, `evaluate`, `done`). Resumed on session restart.
131	- `iteration`: count of iterations attempted so far (accepted + rejected).
132	- `baseline.cycles` / `baseline.instructions`: unmodified library measurement. Reference point for speedup.
133	- `best.cycles` / `best.instructions`: measurements from the winning iteration.
134	- `best.iteration`: which `history` entry is the winner.
135	- `best.commit`: SHA of the commit holding the winning edit of `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`. Required -- if the `.o` cache is evicted, check this SHA out and rebuild with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` to regenerate the exact artifact. Also the anchor for `git revert` on reject.
136	- `history[i].iteration`: monotonic integer, one per attempt.
137	- `history[i].commit`: SHA of that attempt's commit. Required for the same reason as `best.commit`.
138	- `history[i].cycles` / `instructions`: measured counts. Comparable only within the same (function, shape-set, target).
139	- `history[i].accepted`: `true` only if accuracy passed AND `cycles < best.cycles` at that moment. Rejected entries stay so future sessions don't retry the same idea.
140	- `history[i].notes`: one-line description of the idea.
141	
142	Best selection: `best = argmin(cycles)` over `history` entries with `accepted=true`; `best.commit` points at the source to restore.
143	
144	## Optimization Loop
145	
146	The LLM executes this loop directly, calling shell scripts at each mechanical step:
147	
148	### Phase 1 -- `gather`
149	
150	Parse the task spec and target features. No builds yet.
151	
152	1. Read `opt/config.toml`. Resolve toolchain `PATH`, QEMU binary, FPGA `host:port`, target CPU, VLEN.
153	2. Read the FPGA `.inc` file. Extract:
154	   - `NDS_VLEN`, `NDS_DLEN`, `NDS_ELEN`
155	   - `NDS_DCACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_DCACHE_WAY`, `NDS_ICACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_CACHE_LINE_SIZE`
156	   - `NDS_L2C_CACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_L2C_WAY`
157	   - `NDS_RVV_SUPPORT`, `NDS_VECTOR_VDOT_SUPPORT`, `NDS_INT4_VECTOR_LOAD_SUPPORT`, `NDS_BFLOAT16_SUPPORT`, `NDS_QMAC_SUPPORT`
158	   - `NDS_BIU_DATA_WIDTH`, `NDS_L2_DATA_WIDTH`
159	3. Read `state.source_input`:
160	   - **Single mode:** parse `#define` block -> shapes, quant params. Resolve target function name.
161	   - **Model mode:** parse the model `.c` and included param headers -> ordered `(function, shape-set)` list. Deduplicate on (name, shape-set).
162	4. Locate each function's baseline `.c` under `/home/nick/work/libnn/Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`. Record path in state.
163	5. Write everything to `state.json`. Advance `phase -> "baseline"`.
164	
165	**Verifiable test:** running phase 1 on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c` produces a state file with the exact shapes from the `#define` block and `target_features.vlen == 1024`, `target_features.dcache_kb == 32`, `target_features.l2_kb == 8192`.
166	
167	### Phase 2 -- `baseline`
168	
169	Measure unmodified library on the target.
170	
171	1. Rebuild baseline `libnn.a`:
172	   ```
173	   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/build.sh <cpu>
174	   ```
175	2. QEMU correctness sanity (fast, fd-9 strace mode):
176	   ```
177	   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh <test> --strace --vlen <vlen>
178	   ```
179	   Accept only `accuracy checking pass`. Failures here abort the run -- baseline is broken and no delta is meaningful.
180	3. FPGA perf measurement. For each `(function, shape-set)`:
181	   ```
182	   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/test_perf.sh ax45mpv riscv64-elf-gcc BOARD <function>
183	   ```
184	   Parse `performance.csv` -> `{cycles, instructions}`.
185	4. Write `baseline` and initialize empty `history`. Advance `phase -> "iterate"`.
186	
187	
188	### Phase 3 -- `iterate`
189	
190	One iteration = one candidate optimization. Loop runs until `max_iterations` or no improvement >= `min_improvement_pct` for 2 consecutive attempts.
191	
192	Per iteration:
193	
194	1. **Plan.** Inspect the current `best` source (or baseline on iter 1). Pick one concrete change (e.g. "tile K dim to fit 32KB D-cache", "LMUL=2 outer, LMUL=1 inner", "vdot accumulator 4-way unroll"). Record in `notes`.
195	2. **Edit.** Modify `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c` in place: add the optimized path under `#ifdef ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` (replacing the prior iteration's `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block if present). The baseline `#else` branch remains untouched byte-for-byte.
196	3. **Build.** Rebuild `libnn.a` twice: once without `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` (sanity -- baseline must still compile and pass) and once with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` for perf. Output goes to `opt/build/libnn.a`.
197	4. **Correctness.** Rerun QEMU strace test. On `fail` -> mark `accepted=false`, skip perf, continue.
198	5. **Perf.** Rerun `test_perf.sh` pointing at `opt/build/libnn.a`. Collect cycles/instructions.
199	6. **Commit.** `git -C /home/nick/work/libnn add Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c && git commit -m "[opt/rvv] iter N: <notes>"`. Record SHA. Also snapshot the diff to `opt/patches/iter_<N>_<func>.patch` for the audit trail.
200	7. **Accept/reject.** `accepted = pass && cycles < best.cycles`. Update `best` if accepted.
201	8. Append to `history`. Persist `state.json`.
202	
203	**Verifiable test:** after a forced-regression iteration (e.g. add a `nop` loop), `history[N].accepted == false` and `best` is unchanged.
204	
205	### Phase 4 -- `evaluate`
206	
207	1. Restore `best.commit` in the libnn worktree -- its edit to `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c` is the winning artifact.
208	2. Rerun full QEMU suite for touched functions -- catch shape-specialization bugs that the single perf test missed.
209	3. Rerun FPGA perf one final time, confirm it matches `best.cycles` within +/- 0.5%.
210	4. **Model mode only:** re-profile every `(function, shape-set)` pair so total model cycles reflect all accepted winners simultaneously.
211	5. Emit `opt/report.md`: per-pair baseline -> best cycles, speedup, winning commit SHA (in libnn), one-line rationale.
212	6. Advance `phase -> "done"`.
213	
214	
215	
216	**Verifiable test:** `opt/report.md` exists, each row's `speedup` equals `baseline/best`, and model-mode total matches the sum of pair totals.
217	
218	### Phase 5 -- `done`
219	
220	Terminal. Human reviews `opt/report.md`. If accepted, the winning commits in the libnn tree (the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`-guarded edits) are the deliverable; promoting the new path out from under the macro (or merging it into the default branch) is out of scope for this workflow.
221	
222	## Implementation Steps (for Claude Code)
223	
224	Each step is one feature, has a verifiable test, and declares dependencies. Steps are ordered; later steps depend on earlier ones unless noted.
225	
226	### Step 1 -- Config loader
227	
228	- **Feature:** read `opt/config.toml`, expose fields as a Python dict (or shell env export).
229	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/load_config.sh` (emits `eval`-able `KEY=VALUE` lines).
230	- **Test:** `load_config.sh opt/config.toml` prints `TOOLCHAIN_BIN=...`, `QEMU=...`, `FPGA_HOST=...`, `FPGA_PORT=...`, `CPU=AX45MPV`, `VLEN=1024`. Exits non-zero on missing keys.
231	- **Depends on:** none.
232	
233	### Step 2 -- FPGA `.inc` parser
234	
235	- **Feature:** extract hardware features from a Verilog `.inc` file into JSON.
236	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_fpga_inc.sh` -> stdout JSON.
237	- **Test:** parsing `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc` yields `{"vlen":1024,"dlen":1024,"dcache_kb":32,"dcache_way":4,"l2_kb":8192,"cache_line":64,"rvv":true,"vdot":true,"bf16":true,"int4_vload":true}`.
238	- **Depends on:** none.
239	
240	### Step 3 -- Perf-test spec parser (single mode)
241	
242	- **Feature:** given a `t_pf_*.c`, extract function name + `#define` shapes + quant params as JSON.
243	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_perf_test.sh`.
244	- **Test:** on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c`, JSON contains `function == "conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any"` and all INPUT_/KERNEL_/STRIDE_/PAD_ defines.
245	- **Depends on:** none.
246	
247	### Step 4 -- Model-spec parser (model mode)
248	
249	- **Feature:** given a model `.c`, walk `riscv_nn_*` call sites and their param headers -> ordered list of `(function, shape-set)` with call counts.
250	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_model.sh`.
251	- **Test:** on `Examples/ARM/arm_nn_examples/cifar10/nds_nn_examples_cifar10.c`, produces the 11-row table in "Mode 2" above.
252	- **Depends on:** none.
253	
254	### Step 5 -- Library build with `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`
255	
256	- **Feature:** rebuild `libnn.a` from the libnn tree with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` so the macro-guarded optimized path is compiled in place of the baseline branch.
257	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/build_with_agent_opt.sh`.
258	  Implementation: `EXTRA_FLAGS="-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]" /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/build.sh <cpu> "$EXTRA_FLAGS"`, then copy `libnn.a` to `opt/build/libnn.a`. Also runs a baseline build (no macro) for the sanity check called out in Phase 3 step 3.
259	- **Test:** `nm opt/build/libnn.a | grep <func>` matches baseline symbol name; objdump of that object differs from a baseline build.
260	- **Depends on:** Step 1.
261	
262	### Step 6 -- QEMU correctness runner
263	
264	- **Feature:** run the `.c` test through `qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh --strace`, return pass/fail.
265	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/check_correctness.sh <test_name>`.
266	- **Test:** returns 0 on `relu_s8`, non-zero when the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` branch in `Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c` is intentionally broken.
267	- **Depends on:** Step 5.
268	
269	### Step 7 -- FPGA perf runner
270	
271	- **Feature:** run `test_perf.sh` against `opt/build/libnn.a`, parse `performance.csv`.
272	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/measure_fpga.sh <function>` -> JSON `{"cycles":N,"instructions":M}`.
273	- **Test:** two consecutive runs on the unmodified baseline agree within +/- 1%.
274	- **Depends on:** Steps 1, 5.
275	
276	### Step 8 -- State I/O
277	
278	- **Feature:** atomic read/write of `opt/state.json`.
279	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/state_get.sh <key>`, `opt/scripts/state_set.sh <key> <json>`.
280	- **Test:** set/get round-trip preserves nested fields; concurrent writes don't corrupt the file (`.tmp` + rename).
281	- **Depends on:** none.
282	
283	### Step 9 -- Phase driver: `gather`
284	
285	- **Feature:** implement Phase 1 end-to-end.
286	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_gather.sh`.
287	- **Test:** running on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c` with `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc` produces a state file matching Phase 1's verifiable-test assertion.
288	- **Depends on:** Steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 8.
289	
290	### Step 10 -- Phase driver: `baseline`
291	
292	- **Feature:** implement Phase 2.
293	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_baseline.sh`.
294	- **Test:** state file gains populated `baseline.cycles`/`baseline.instructions`; cycles match historical baseline CSV within 1%.
295	- **Depends on:** Steps 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.
296	
297	### Step 11 -- Phase driver: `iterate` (one step)
298	
299	- **Feature:** run a single iteration given that the LLM has already edited the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block in `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`.
300	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_iterate_once.sh`.
301	- **Responsibilities:** build with and without `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`, correctness, perf, commit, update `history`/`best`.
302	- **Test:** injecting a known-good hand-optimized kernel for `relu_s8` produces `accepted=true` and lowers `best.cycles`; injecting a broken kernel produces `accepted=false` with `best` unchanged.
303	- **Depends on:** Steps 5, 6, 7, 8, 10.
304	
305	### Step 12 -- Phase driver: `evaluate`
306	
307	- **Feature:** implement Phase 4.
308	- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_evaluate.sh`, report template `opt/scripts/report.md.tmpl`.
309	- **Test:** `opt/report.md` is generated and the computed speedup equals `baseline.cycles / best.cycles` per pair.
310	- **Depends on:** Steps 6, 7, 8, 11.
311	
312	### Step 13 -- LLM-facing loop prompt
313	
314	- **Feature:** a single Markdown runbook (`opt/RUNBOOK.md`) that tells the LLM, given the current `state.json`, which phase script to call next and what inputs it needs.
315	- **Files:** `opt/RUNBOOK.md`.
316	- **Test:** feeding a synthetic `state.json` with `phase="iterate"` and an LLM-written edit to the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block results in the LLM invoking `phase_iterate_once.sh` and nothing else.
317	- **Depends on:** Steps 9, 10, 11, 12.
318	
319	### Step 14 -- End-to-end smoke
320	
321	- **Feature:** full run from zero state to `done` on a small function.
322	- **Files:** `opt/tests/smoke_relu_s8.sh`.
323	- **Test:** starting from an empty `opt/` (sans config), a scripted LLM loop on `t_pf_relu_any_s8.c` terminates in `done`, `best.cycles <= baseline.cycles`, and `opt/report.md` exists.
324	- **Depends on:** Steps 1-13.
325	
326	## Open Questions (resolve before Step 1)
327	
328	- Does `test_perf.sh` support pointing at an alternate `libnn.a`, or must we stage `opt/build/libnn.a` at the repo root before invocation? Needed for Step 7.
329	- For model mode, are the param headers always co-located with the model `.c`, or sometimes under a shared include path? Affects Step 4.
330	- FPGA access: is `MYPC`/`MYPORT` already exported by `test_perf.sh`, or do we need `opt/config.toml` to override? Affects Step 7.
331

file.content:
# Implementation Plan: Autonomous Agentic libnn Optimization Workflow

## Context

libnn functions have multi-variant C implementations (plain C / DSP / RVV). Optimizing a function means writing a better RVV implementation for a specific FPGA target. Currently this is manual: read source, write code, build, test on QEMU, profile on FPGA, iterate.

This plan defines how **Claude Code directly drives the optimization loop** -- no Python orchestration framework. The LLM reads source files, parses hardware configs, writes optimized C code, and calls existing shell scripts for build/test/profile. For state persistence across sessions, a simple JSON file tracks progress.

The optimization is specialized for a **designated target configuration** (CPU, VLEN, cache hierarchy, ISA extensions) and **known static-time input shapes** (batch size, spatial dimensions, channel counts, kernel sizes, etc.) extracted from existing test/example C files. Dynamic shapes are not supported -- each optimization run targets a fixed set of input dimensions, enabling aggressive tiling, unrolling, and register allocation decisions that would not be valid for arbitrary shapes.

**The baseline code path is never modified.** Instead, the agent edits the same source file (e.g., `/home/nick/work/libnn/Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c`) and adds one or more optimized paths, each guarded by its own macro. The baseline path remains byte-for-byte intact under `#else`. A/B comparison is a rebuild with or without the relevant `-D` flag.

Macro naming:
- **Single mode:** one macro per function, `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>` (e.g. `ENA_AGENT_OPT_CONV_DW_HWC_U8_U8_U8_ASYM_BIAS_ANY`).
- **Model mode:** the same function can appear multiple times with different shape-sets, and each shape-set gets its own specialized path. Macros are suffixed by shape-set index: `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>_S0`, `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>_S1`, ... Dispatch at the top of the function picks the right specialization by comparing the incoming shape arguments against the compile-time constants of each shape-set; if none match, fall through to the baseline `#else`. The index-to-shape-set mapping is recorded in `state.json` so the build flags and dispatcher stay in sync.

The FPGA configuration (Verilog `.inc` file like `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc`) provides the ground truth for target features: VLEN, DLEN, L2 cache size, ISA extensions, etc. Cache sizes are especially important since the library's tiling GEMM currently hardcodes L1=512KB -- the actual L2=8MB and L1 D-cache=32KB from the config should drive tiling parameter selection.

## Two Modes of Operation

### Mode 1: Single Function

**Input:** A performance test file from `Examples/unit_performance/`, e.g.:
```
Examples/unit_performance/t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c
```

The LLM reads this file and extracts:
- **Function name** from the `riscv_nn_*()` call site
- **All static input shapes** from the `#define` macros at the top of the file (`INPUT_X=16`, `INPUT_Y=15`, `INPUT_CH=4`, `KERNEL_X=2`, `CH_MULT=192`, `STRIDE_X=1`, `PAD_X=0`, etc.)
- **Quantization parameters** (`OUT_SHIFT`, `OUT_MULT`, `INPUT_OFFSET`, `FILTER_OFFSET`, `OUTPUT_OFFSET`, activation min/max)
- **Data types** inferred from the function name suffix (`u8_u8_u8`, `s8_s8_s8`, etc.)

No separate TOML or config file needed -- the perf test file *is* the task specification.

### Mode 2: Complete Model

**Input:** A model example C file, e.g.:
```
Examples/ARM/arm_nn_examples/cifar10/nds_nn_examples_cifar10.c
```

The LLM reads the model file and its companion parameter header (found via `#include`) to extract the full op sequence with concrete shapes:

| # | Function | Key shapes |
|---|----------|-----------|
| 1 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_RGB_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=32, im_ch=3, ker=5, out_ch=32, stride=1, pad=2 |
| 2 | `relu_s8` | size=32*32*32 |
| 3 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=32, ch=32, ker=3, stride=2 |
| 4 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=16, im_ch=32, ker=5, out_ch=16 |
| 5 | `relu_s8` | size=16*16*16 |
| 6 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=16, ch=16, ker=3, stride=2 |
| 7 | `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | im_dim=8, im_ch=16, ker=5, out_ch=32 |
| 8 | `relu_s8` | size=8*8*32 |
| 9 | `maxpool_HWC_s8` | im_dim=8, ch=32, ker=3, stride=2 |
| 10 | `fc_s8_s8_s8_sft_bias_fast` | in_dim=512, out=10 |
| 11 | `softmax_s8_fast` | size=10 |

The LLM profiles all (function, shape-set) pairs and optimizes every one of them to reach the best total model cycle count. Cost per pair is measured directly -- baseline cycles at that exact shape, multiplied by how many times the model invokes the function with that shape. Same function with two different shape sets = two independent pairs with independent costs (a 32x32 conv and an 8x8 conv share a name but not a cycle budget). Ranking by per-pair total cycles only sets work *order* so the largest wins land first and model-level cycles drop monotonically; it does not gate coverage. The long tail is still specialized -- a small kernel invoked many times can dominate once the heavy convs are tuned.

## Directory Structure

```
agentic_libnn_opt/
  plan.md                                   # This file
  opt/
    config.toml                             # Target config (toolchain, FPGA, QEMU paths)
    state.json                              # Optimization state (persists across sessions)
    patches/                                # Per-iteration diffs against libnn Source/ (audit trail)
      iter_<N>_<func>.patch
    log/                                    # Build/test/perf logs per iteration
  # The agent's macro for the new path:
  #   -DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]            enables the optimized code path in Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c
  ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc              # FPGA Verilog config (input)
```

## Configuration

Single TOML file at `opt/config.toml` -- read directly by the LLM, no schema validation layer needed:

```toml
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix = "riscv64-elf-"

[target]
cpu = "AX45MPV"
fpga_config = "ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc"

[qemu]
binary = "/local/nick/qemu_andes/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
vlen = 1024
timeout = 120

[fpga]
host = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port = 1116
timeout = 300

[limits]
max_iterations = 10
min_improvement_pct = 2.0
```

## State File

`opt/state.json` -- simple JSON the LLM reads/writes to track progress across sessions:

```json
{
  "mode": "single_function",
  "function": "conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any",
  "source_input": "Examples/unit_performance/t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c",
  "phase": "evaluate",
  "iteration": 3,
  "baseline": { "cycles": 45200, "instructions": 32100 },
  "best": { "cycles": 38900, "instructions": 28500, "iteration": 2, "commit": "e4f5a6b" },
  "history": [
    { "iteration": 1, "commit": "a1b2c3d", "cycles": 42000, "instructions": 30200, "accepted": true,  "notes": "unrolled inner loop 4x" },
    { "iteration": 2, "commit": "e4f5a6b", "cycles": 38900, "instructions": 28500, "accepted": true,  "notes": "tiled for L1 dcache 32KB" },
    { "iteration": 3, "commit": "9c8d7e6", "cycles": 39100, "instructions": 27800, "accepted": false, "notes": "register pressure regression" }
  ]
}
```

Field semantics:
- `mode`: `"single_function"` or `"model"`. Drives which loop variant runs.
- `function`: bare function name (no `riscv_nn_` prefix). Identifies which baseline `.c` gets the new macro-guarded path.
- `source_input`: path to the perf test `.c` (single mode) or model `.c` (model mode). The task spec.
- `phase`: current loop phase (`gather`, `baseline`, `iterate`, `evaluate`, `done`). Resumed on session restart.
- `iteration`: count of iterations attempted so far (accepted + rejected).
- `baseline.cycles` / `baseline.instructions`: unmodified library measurement. Reference point for speedup.
- `best.cycles` / `best.instructions`: measurements from the winning iteration.
- `best.iteration`: which `history` entry is the winner.
- `best.commit`: SHA of the commit holding the winning edit of `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`. Required -- if the `.o` cache is evicted, check this SHA out and rebuild with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` to regenerate the exact artifact. Also the anchor for `git revert` on reject.
- `history[i].iteration`: monotonic integer, one per attempt.
- `history[i].commit`: SHA of that attempt's commit. Required for the same reason as `best.commit`.
- `history[i].cycles` / `instructions`: measured counts. Comparable only within the same (function, shape-set, target).
- `history[i].accepted`: `true` only if accuracy passed AND `cycles < best.cycles` at that moment. Rejected entries stay so future sessions don't retry the same idea.
- `history[i].notes`: one-line description of the idea.

Best selection: `best = argmin(cycles)` over `history` entries with `accepted=true`; `best.commit` points at the source to restore.

## Optimization Loop

The LLM executes this loop directly, calling shell scripts at each mechanical step:

### Phase 1 -- `gather`

Parse the task spec and target features. No builds yet.

1. Read `opt/config.toml`. Resolve toolchain `PATH`, QEMU binary, FPGA `host:port`, target CPU, VLEN.
2. Read the FPGA `.inc` file. Extract:
   - `NDS_VLEN`, `NDS_DLEN`, `NDS_ELEN`
   - `NDS_DCACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_DCACHE_WAY`, `NDS_ICACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_CACHE_LINE_SIZE`
   - `NDS_L2C_CACHE_SIZE_KB`, `NDS_L2C_WAY`
   - `NDS_RVV_SUPPORT`, `NDS_VECTOR_VDOT_SUPPORT`, `NDS_INT4_VECTOR_LOAD_SUPPORT`, `NDS_BFLOAT16_SUPPORT`, `NDS_QMAC_SUPPORT`
   - `NDS_BIU_DATA_WIDTH`, `NDS_L2_DATA_WIDTH`
3. Read `state.source_input`:
   - **Single mode:** parse `#define` block -> shapes, quant params. Resolve target function name.
   - **Model mode:** parse the model `.c` and included param headers -> ordered `(function, shape-set)` list. Deduplicate on (name, shape-set).
4. Locate each function's baseline `.c` under `/home/nick/work/libnn/Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`. Record path in state.
5. Write everything to `state.json`. Advance `phase -> "baseline"`.

**Verifiable test:** running phase 1 on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c` produces a state file with the exact shapes from the `#define` block and `target_features.vlen == 1024`, `target_features.dcache_kb == 32`, `target_features.l2_kb == 8192`.

### Phase 2 -- `baseline`

Measure unmodified library on the target.

1. Rebuild baseline `libnn.a`:
   ```
   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/build.sh <cpu>
   ```
2. QEMU correctness sanity (fast, fd-9 strace mode):
   ```
   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh <test> --strace --vlen <vlen>
   ```
   Accept only `accuracy checking pass`. Failures here abort the run -- baseline is broken and no delta is meaningful.
3. FPGA perf measurement. For each `(function, shape-set)`:
   ```
   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/test_perf.sh ax45mpv riscv64-elf-gcc BOARD <function>
   ```
   Parse `performance.csv` -> `{cycles, instructions}`.
4. Write `baseline` and initialize empty `history`. Advance `phase -> "iterate"`.


### Phase 3 -- `iterate`

One iteration = one candidate optimization. Loop runs until `max_iterations` or no improvement >= `min_improvement_pct` for 2 consecutive attempts.

Per iteration:

1. **Plan.** Inspect the current `best` source (or baseline on iter 1). Pick one concrete change (e.g. "tile K dim to fit 32KB D-cache", "LMUL=2 outer, LMUL=1 inner", "vdot accumulator 4-way unroll"). Record in `notes`.
2. **Edit.** Modify `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c` in place: add the optimized path under `#ifdef ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` (replacing the prior iteration's `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block if present). The baseline `#else` branch remains untouched byte-for-byte.
3. **Build.** Rebuild `libnn.a` twice: once without `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` (sanity -- baseline must still compile and pass) and once with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` for perf. Output goes to `opt/build/libnn.a`.
4. **Correctness.** Rerun QEMU strace test. On `fail` -> mark `accepted=false`, skip perf, continue.
5. **Perf.** Rerun `test_perf.sh` pointing at `opt/build/libnn.a`. Collect cycles/instructions.
6. **Commit.** `git -C /home/nick/work/libnn add Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c && git commit -m "[opt/rvv] iter N: <notes>"`. Record SHA. Also snapshot the diff to `opt/patches/iter_<N>_<func>.patch` for the audit trail.
7. **Accept/reject.** `accepted = pass && cycles < best.cycles`. Update `best` if accepted.
8. Append to `history`. Persist `state.json`.

**Verifiable test:** after a forced-regression iteration (e.g. add a `nop` loop), `history[N].accepted == false` and `best` is unchanged.

### Phase 4 -- `evaluate`

1. Restore `best.commit` in the libnn worktree -- its edit to `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c` is the winning artifact.
2. Rerun full QEMU suite for touched functions -- catch shape-specialization bugs that the single perf test missed.
3. Rerun FPGA perf one final time, confirm it matches `best.cycles` within +/- 0.5%.
4. **Model mode only:** re-profile every `(function, shape-set)` pair so total model cycles reflect all accepted winners simultaneously.
5. Emit `opt/report.md`: per-pair baseline -> best cycles, speedup, winning commit SHA (in libnn), one-line rationale.
6. Advance `phase -> "done"`.



**Verifiable test:** `opt/report.md` exists, each row's `speedup` equals `baseline/best`, and model-mode total matches the sum of pair totals.

### Phase 5 -- `done`

Terminal. Human reviews `opt/report.md`. If accepted, the winning commits in the libnn tree (the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`-guarded edits) are the deliverable; promoting the new path out from under the macro (or merging it into the default branch) is out of scope for this workflow.

## Implementation Steps (for Claude Code)

Each step is one feature, has a verifiable test, and declares dependencies. Steps are ordered; later steps depend on earlier ones unless noted.

### Step 1 -- Config loader

- **Feature:** read `opt/config.toml`, expose fields as a Python dict (or shell env export).
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/load_config.sh` (emits `eval`-able `KEY=VALUE` lines).
- **Test:** `load_config.sh opt/config.toml` prints `TOOLCHAIN_BIN=...`, `QEMU=...`, `FPGA_HOST=...`, `FPGA_PORT=...`, `CPU=AX45MPV`, `VLEN=1024`. Exits non-zero on missing keys.
- **Depends on:** none.

### Step 2 -- FPGA `.inc` parser

- **Feature:** extract hardware features from a Verilog `.inc` file into JSON.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_fpga_inc.sh` -> stdout JSON.
- **Test:** parsing `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc` yields `{"vlen":1024,"dlen":1024,"dcache_kb":32,"dcache_way":4,"l2_kb":8192,"cache_line":64,"rvv":true,"vdot":true,"bf16":true,"int4_vload":true}`.
- **Depends on:** none.

### Step 3 -- Perf-test spec parser (single mode)

- **Feature:** given a `t_pf_*.c`, extract function name + `#define` shapes + quant params as JSON.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_perf_test.sh`.
- **Test:** on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c`, JSON contains `function == "conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any"` and all INPUT_/KERNEL_/STRIDE_/PAD_ defines.
- **Depends on:** none.

### Step 4 -- Model-spec parser (model mode)

- **Feature:** given a model `.c`, walk `riscv_nn_*` call sites and their param headers -> ordered list of `(function, shape-set)` with call counts.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/parse_model.sh`.
- **Test:** on `Examples/ARM/arm_nn_examples/cifar10/nds_nn_examples_cifar10.c`, produces the 11-row table in "Mode 2" above.
- **Depends on:** none.

### Step 5 -- Library build with `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`

- **Feature:** rebuild `libnn.a` from the libnn tree with `-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` so the macro-guarded optimized path is compiled in place of the baseline branch.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/build_with_agent_opt.sh`.
  Implementation: `EXTRA_FLAGS="-DENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]" /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/build.sh <cpu> "$EXTRA_FLAGS"`, then copy `libnn.a` to `opt/build/libnn.a`. Also runs a baseline build (no macro) for the sanity check called out in Phase 3 step 3.
- **Test:** `nm opt/build/libnn.a | grep <func>` matches baseline symbol name; objdump of that object differs from a baseline build.
- **Depends on:** Step 1.

### Step 6 -- QEMU correctness runner

- **Feature:** run the `.c` test through `qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh --strace`, return pass/fail.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/check_correctness.sh <test_name>`.
- **Test:** returns 0 on `relu_s8`, non-zero when the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` branch in `Source/ActivationFunctions/riscv_nn_relu_s8.c` is intentionally broken.
- **Depends on:** Step 5.

### Step 7 -- FPGA perf runner

- **Feature:** run `test_perf.sh` against `opt/build/libnn.a`, parse `performance.csv`.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/measure_fpga.sh <function>` -> JSON `{"cycles":N,"instructions":M}`.
- **Test:** two consecutive runs on the unmodified baseline agree within +/- 1%.
- **Depends on:** Steps 1, 5.

### Step 8 -- State I/O

- **Feature:** atomic read/write of `opt/state.json`.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/state_get.sh <key>`, `opt/scripts/state_set.sh <key> <json>`.
- **Test:** set/get round-trip preserves nested fields; concurrent writes don't corrupt the file (`.tmp` + rename).
- **Depends on:** none.

### Step 9 -- Phase driver: `gather`

- **Feature:** implement Phase 1 end-to-end.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_gather.sh`.
- **Test:** running on `t_pf_conv_dw_HWC_u8_u8_u8_asym_bias_any.c` with `ax45mpv_v1024_8mb_config.inc` produces a state file matching Phase 1's verifiable-test assertion.
- **Depends on:** Steps 1, 2, 3, 4, 8.

### Step 10 -- Phase driver: `baseline`

- **Feature:** implement Phase 2.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_baseline.sh`.
- **Test:** state file gains populated `baseline.cycles`/`baseline.instructions`; cycles match historical baseline CSV within 1%.
- **Depends on:** Steps 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.

### Step 11 -- Phase driver: `iterate` (one step)

- **Feature:** run a single iteration given that the LLM has already edited the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block in `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func>.c`.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_iterate_once.sh`.
- **Responsibilities:** build with and without `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]`, correctness, perf, commit, update `history`/`best`.
- **Test:** injecting a known-good hand-optimized kernel for `relu_s8` produces `accepted=true` and lowers `best.cycles`; injecting a broken kernel produces `accepted=false` with `best` unchanged.
- **Depends on:** Steps 5, 6, 7, 8, 10.

### Step 12 -- Phase driver: `evaluate`

- **Feature:** implement Phase 4.
- **Files:** `opt/scripts/phase_evaluate.sh`, report template `opt/scripts/report.md.tmpl`.
- **Test:** `opt/report.md` is generated and the computed speedup equals `baseline.cycles / best.cycles` per pair.
- **Depends on:** Steps 6, 7, 8, 11.

### Step 13 -- LLM-facing loop prompt

- **Feature:** a single Markdown runbook (`opt/RUNBOOK.md`) that tells the LLM, given the current `state.json`, which phase script to call next and what inputs it needs.
- **Files:** `opt/RUNBOOK.md`.
- **Test:** feeding a synthetic `state.json` with `phase="iterate"` and an LLM-written edit to the `ENA_AGENT_OPT_<FUNC>[_S<N>]` block results in the LLM invoking `phase_iterate_once.sh` and nothing else.
- **Depends on:** Steps 9, 10, 11, 12.

### Step 14 -- End-to-end smoke

- **Feature:** full run from zero state to `done` on a small function.
- **Files:** `opt/tests/smoke_relu_s8.sh`.
- **Test:** starting from an empty `opt/` (sans config), a scripted LLM loop on `t_pf_relu_any_s8.c` terminates in `done`, `best.cycles <= baseline.cycles`, and `opt/report.md` exists.
- **Depends on:** Steps 1-13.

## Open Questions (resolve before Step 1)

- Does `test_perf.sh` support pointing at an alternate `libnn.a`, or must we stage `opt/build/libnn.a` at the repo root before invocation? Needed for Step 7.
- For model mode, are the param headers always co-located with the model `.c`, or sometimes under a shared include path? Affects Step 4.
- FPGA access: is `MYPC`/`MYPORT` already exported by `test_perf.sh`, or do we need `opt/config.toml` to override? Affects Step 7.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: optimize
3	description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions on AX45MPV (VLEN=1024). Runs a 7-step FSM — select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, profile, learn — then loops back to analyze with new pipeline insights. No human intervention required.
4	argument-hint: [function_name|status|reset] [algorithm|uarch]
5	disable-model-invocation: false
6	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
7	---
8	
9	# libnn RVV Optimization Skill
10	
11	Fully autonomous optimization loop. State persisted in `.claude/opt-state.json`.
12	
13	## Current State
14	
15	!`cat /home/nick/work/libnn/.claude/opt-state.json 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`
16	
17	## Commands
18	
19	- `/optimize <function_name> [algorithm|uarch]` — Start optimizing a function (loops until flat land). `algorithm` (default) starts at algorithm-level and escalates to micro-arch when exhausted. `uarch` starts directly at micro-architecture-level, skipping algorithm-level analysis.
20	- `/optimize status` — Show current state
21	- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle
22	
23	Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`
24	
25	## Finite State Machine
26	
27	```
28	idle → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
29	                  ↑ |          ↑          |              |              |
30	                  | |          └──────────┘              │ (uarch only) |
31	                  | |       (verify fail → fix → retry)  ↓              |
32	                  | |                                  profile ─────────┘
33	                  | |                                                    |
34	                  | └→ idle (no opportunity found)                       |
35	                  |                                                      |
36	                  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
37	                    (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
38	
39	analyze internal flow:
40	  ┌─ check optimization_level from state ──────────────────┐
41	  │                                                         │
42	  │ "algorithm" (default):        "micro-arch" (uarch arg): │
43	  │  proposer → idea → implement  proposer → idea → implement
44	  │          → no idea → idle             → no idea → idle  │
45	  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
46	
47	Exit conditions:
48	  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity at current level → idle
49	  - max 5 rounds per function → idle
50	```
51	
52	The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from the pipeline profile and kernel assembly. Each round builds on what was learned. The `analyze` step operates at the level set by the `optimization_level` argument — `algorithm` (default) for data flow, tiling, GEMM strategy, or `micro-arch` (via `uarch` argument) for scheduling, LMUL tuning, stalls. There is no implicit escalation between levels. The verify/measure/profile loop provides the real feedback.
53	
54	---
55	
56	## Build Scripts Reference
57	
58	All scripts are at `/home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/`. Always use `/usr/bin/bash` to invoke them.
59	
60	| Script | Purpose | Time |
61	|--------|---------|------|
62	| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files). Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
63	| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
64	| qemu-runner subagent | QEMU correctness test (handles fd 9 semihosting). Compile test binary first, then dispatch agent. | ~1s |
65	| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. Produces .adx and .objdump for vsim. | ~30s |
66	| `run_vsim_kernel.sh <kern.S> [--log-cycles N]` | vsim pipeline from standalone kernel assembly extract. No fast-forward. | ~5s |
67	| `run_vsim_profile.sh <test> <start> <end> [--log-cycles N]` | vsim pipeline from real test binary with fast-forward. Fallback. | ~minutes |
68	
69	---
70	
71	## State: `idle`
72	
73	No optimization in progress.
74	
75	If a function name is provided, transition → `select`.
76	If "status", report idle. If "reset", clear state.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## State: `select`
81	
82	Validate the function and gather baseline context.
83

Otherwise default to `optimization_level="algorithm"`. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, called_functions, optimization_level.

112	---
113	
114	## State: `analyze`
115	
116	Study the current RVV code, assembly, and pipeline data to form the next optimization hypothesis.
117	
118	**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from the COMPILED BINARY, not from source `#ifdef` guards.**
119	
120	Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from source alone.** Instead:
121	
122	1. Check the linked test binary's objdump for what functions are actually called:
123	   ```bash
124	   # Look at the perf objdump from the select step's FPGA run
125	   grep "jal.*<" qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
126	   ```
127	2. Cross-reference the called sub-functions (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) back to the source to identify which `#if/#elif` block is active.
128	3. Read only the ACTIVE block. Modifying dead code has zero effect.
129	
130	**Key defines active for AX45MPV** (from build.sh + internal headers):
131	- `ENA_VEC_ISA` — from build.sh
132	- `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000` — from build.sh
133	- `ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD` — from build.sh
134	- `ENA_TILING` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h:2256`
135	- `ENA_FAST_ALGO` — from `internal/internal_nn_math.h`
136	- `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` — **NOT defined** (dead code)
137	- `ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC` — **NOT defined** (unless toolchain provides it implicitly for some files)
138	
139	### Two Optimization Levels
140	
141	The analyze step operates at two levels, tried in order:
142	
143	1. **Algorithm-level** (default) — data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization (elements per iteration, register blocking)
144	2. **Micro-architecture-level** — instruction scheduling, dual-issue, VQ→VD stall reduction, VLSU prefetching, software pipelining (assumes LMUL and algorithm are fixed)
145	
146	The level is fixed at invocation time via the `uarch` argument. There is no implicit escalation from algorithm to micro-arch.
147	
148	#### Flow within analyze
149	
150	```
151	analyze entry
152	  → read optimization_level from state
153	  → if "algorithm":
154	      → launch proposer subagent (algorithm focus)
155	      → if proposer picks an idea → proceed to implement
156	      → if proposer says "no algorithm opportunity" → transition to idle
157	  → if "micro-arch":
158	      → launch proposer subagent (micro-arch focus)
159	      → if proposer picks an idea → proceed to implement
160	      → if proposer says "no micro-arch opportunity" → transition to idle
161	```
162	
163	### Algorithm-Level Analysis (optimization_level = "algorithm")
164	
165	**On first round (round=1):**
166	1. Identify the active code path using the linked binary's objdump. Read that specific block in the source.
167	2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump`:
168	   ```bash
169	   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' /home/nick/work/libnn/libnn.objdump | head -300
170	   ```
171	3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), also read those sub-functions' assembly and source
172	4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
173	5. Identify bottleneck: LMUL choice, tiling, register pressure, memory access, scheduling
174	6. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact
175	
176	**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
177	1. Re-read the source (it changed last round)
178	2. Re-check the objdump for the updated assembly
179	3. Review the pipeline profile from last round's `profile` step (stored in state as `pipeline_insight`)
180	4. Look for NEW bottlenecks revealed by the profile:
181	   - VQ→VD stalls → interleave scalar/vector work
182	   - Too many VW stages → reduce LMUL, increase tiling
183	   - Poor dual-issue → reorder instructions
184	   - VLSU stalls → add prefetch, align data
185	   - Register spills in assembly → reduce register pressure
186	5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
187	6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the profile revealed
188	
189	**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.
190	
191	### Micro-Architecture-Level Analysis (optimization_level = "micro-arch")
192	
193	Only active when `uarch` was explicitly passed as the second argument at invocation.
194	
195	The proposer focuses on:
196	- Pipeline stalls from profile data, instruction scheduling, dual-issue opportunities
197	- VQ→VD stall reduction, VLSU prefetching, register allocation hints, loop software pipelining
198	- LMUL and algorithm are treated as fixed — do not propose LMUL changes or algorithmic restructuring
199	- Must reference specific pipeline stalls from the kanata data or assembly analysis
200	- Must consider whether optimizations survive compiler reordering at -O3
201	
202	**Proposer output:** 1–3 micro-optimizations with pipeline stage justification. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.
203	
204	### Output and Transitions
205	
206	**Output:** Print the analysis summary and hypothesis. Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.
207	
208	**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, pipeline has no obvious stalls, LMUL/tiling are already optimal):
209	- Transition to idle with final summary regardless of level.
210	- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.
211	- Do NOT escalate from algorithm to micro-arch implicitly — level is fixed by the `uarch` argument.
212	
213	**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity at either level)
214	
215	---
216	
217	## State: `implement`
218	
219	Apply the optimization.
220	
221	1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined by cross-referencing the linked binary's function calls with source `#ifdef` blocks. **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards alone** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh.
222	2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
223	3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
224	4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field)
225	5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, revert the regression first, then try the new approach
226	
227	**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
228	The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v1_0.h`.
229	
230	**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is **NOT defined** for AX45MPV builds. Using them causes compilation failures.
231	
232	When writing new RVV code, always:
233	- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to learn the available `NDS_VEC_*` macro names
234	- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
235	- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
236	- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
237	- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)
238	
239	**Transition:** → `verify`

Then launch a NEW subagent with the summary to fix the code. Rebuild and re-verify. Max 3 retries before going back to `analyze` with a different approach.

306	- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check

313	
314	## State: `profile`
315	
316	**Only runs when `optimization_level = "micro-arch"`.**  Skip entirely for `algorithm`-level optimization and go straight to `learn`.
317	
318	
319	Run vsim pipeline analysis to understand WHY performance changed.
320	
321	**Key principle:** Extract the kernel's assembly into a standalone `.S` file and run it directly in vsim. This eliminates the slow fast-forward through test setup/semihosting. The kernel starts executing at cycle 0.
322	
323	### Profiling effort scaling
324	
325	Scale profiling investment based on the magnitude of the current round's result:
326	- **Large change (>10% cycle delta):** Use Approach A (kernel extract) or B (fast-forward) — the detailed pipeline data is worth the effort since the next round builds on it.
327	- **Small change (<10% cycle delta) or neutral/regressed round:** Use Approach C (assembly-only analysis). Spending time on vsim extraction for a marginal round yields diminishing returns. Focus the analysis on understanding WHY the change was small — was it instruction-count-neutral (check `instrs_after` vs previous), or did IPC change? This informs whether the next round should target instruction reduction or pipeline efficiency.
328	
329	### Three profiling approaches
330	
331	**Approach A: Kernel assembly extract (preferred — fast, exact)**
332	
333	1. Extract the hot loop from the perf objdump (`qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump`) — the linked test binary, not `libnn.objdump` (which is the full library and huge)
334	2. Write a standalone `.S` file at `qemu-validate/vsim-bench/kern_<function>.S` that:
335	   - Enables mstatus.VS for the vector unit
336	   - Sets up registers to match the compiler output (pointers, strides, loop bounds)
337	   - Allocates static `.data` buffers sized for 2 loop iterations
338	   - Runs 2 full kernel iterations (e.g., 2 output pixels each doing 3x3=9 vle8+vmax)
339	   - Exits via ecall
340	3. See `qemu-validate/vsim-bench/kern_maxpool_HWC_s8.S` for a working example
341	4. Run with:
342	   ```bash
343	   /usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/run_vsim_kernel.sh \
344	     /home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/vsim-bench/kern_<function>.S \
345	     --log-cycles 2000
346	   ```
347	   This compiles the `.S`, loads it into vsim at `0x10000`, and logs pipeline from cycle 0.
348	   Output: `qemu-validate/work/vsim_kern_<function>.kanata.log`
349	
350	**Approach B: Full test binary with fast-forward (fallback — when kernel is hard to isolate)**
351	
352	Use when the function's control flow is too complex to extract into standalone assembly:
353	```bash
354	# Get function address range from perf objdump
355	grep '<function_name>:' qemu-validate/work/t_<test>_perf.objdump
356	# Run vsim with fast-forward to function range
357	/usr/bin/bash /home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/run_vsim_profile.sh \
358	  <test_name> <func_addr_start> <func_addr_end> --log-cycles 2000
359	```
360	**WARNING:** Fast-forward can take HOURS for test binaries with heavy semihosting I/O (e.g., conv tests with large data). **Always set a 5-minute timeout.** If vsim doesn't complete within 5 minutes, kill it and record `pipeline_insight = "vsim timed out (fast-forward too slow); analysis based on assembly only"`. Then analyze the assembly manually (instruction mix, loop structure, register pressure) instead of relying on pipeline timing.
361	
362	**Approach C: Skip profiling with assembly-only analysis (when both A and B are impractical)**
363	
364	If the kernel is too complex for standalone extraction (Approach A) and fast-forward is too slow (Approach B), perform assembly-only analysis:
365	1. Read the GEMM kernel assembly from the perf objdump
366	2. Count instruction mix: loads, vd4dots, scalar overhead, branches
367	3. Estimate compute-to-load ratio and vector utilization
368	4. Save insight based on assembly structure rather than pipeline timing
369	This is less precise but unblocks the optimization loop.
370	
371	### Parse the kanata pipeline log
372	
373	```bash
374	python3 -c "
375	log = open('/home/nick/work/libnn/qemu-validate/work/vsim_kern_<function>.kanata.log').readlines()
376	instrs = {}
377	cycle = 0
378	for line in log:
379	    line = line.strip()
380	    if line.startswith('C\t'):
381	        cycle += int(line.split('\t')[1])
382	    elif line.startswith('I\t'):
383	        parts = line.split('\t')
384	        iid = int(parts[1])
385	        instrs[iid] = {'label': '', 'stages': {}, 'cycle_issue': cycle, 'extra': []}
386	    elif line.startswith('L\t'):
387	        parts = line.split('\t')
388	        iid = int(parts[1])
389	        if iid in instrs:
390	            lvl = int(parts[2])
391	            text = parts[3] if len(parts) > 3 else ''
392	            if lvl == 0: instrs[iid]['label'] = text.strip()
393	            else: instrs[iid]['extra'].append(text.strip())
394	    elif line.startswith('S\t'):
395	        parts = line.split('\t')
396	        iid = int(parts[1])
397	        if iid in instrs:
398	            lvl = int(parts[2])
399	            stage = parts[3] if len(parts) > 3 else ''
400	            key = f'{stage}' if lvl == 0 else f'v:{stage}'
401	            if key not in instrs[iid]['stages']:
402	                instrs[iid]['stages'][key] = cycle
403	
404	# Print ALL instructions with pipeline stages (* = vector)
405	for iid in sorted(instrs.keys()):
406	    info = instrs[iid]
407	    label = info['label']
408	    stages = info['stages']
409	    stage_str = ' '.join(f'{k}@{v}' for k,v in sorted(stages.items(), key=lambda x:x[1]))
410	    has_vec = bool(info['extra'])
411	    marker = '*' if has_vec else ' '
412	    print(f'{iid:>4} {info[\"cycle_issue\"]:>6} {marker} {label:<55} {stage_str}')
413	"
414	```
415	
416	### Analyze the pipeline
417	
418	Look at the vector instruction pipeline stages:
419	- **VQ→VD→VC→VW** is the VPU pipeline (VW has LMUL sub-stages: VW8→VW1)
420	- **Loop period** = cycle gap between successive iterations of the same instruction
421	- **VW spacing** = gap between VW sub-stages (e.g., 18 cycles = per-element latency)
422	- **VC stalls** = instruction waiting for operand from previous VW completion
423	
424	Key patterns:
425	- **vle8 VW4→VW1**: VLSU writeback, ~4 stages for M4
426	- **vwadd/vmax VW8→VW1**: VALU writeback, 8 stages for M8
427	- **vdiv VW spacing >> 1**: Each VW sub-stage takes many cycles (per-element division)
428	- **vnsra waiting for vdiv**: VW of vnsra can't start until corresponding vdiv VW finishes
429	
430	Save the pipeline insight in state (`pipeline_insight` field).
431	
432	Note: vsim uses VLEN=512. Pipeline patterns (stalls, scheduling) apply identically at VLEN=1024; only element count per instruction changes.
433	
434	**Transition:** → `learn`
435

518	state['prev_step_started'] = state.get('step_started', now.isoformat())
